coredata.dc:title: Determination of carbon nanoparticle dispersion solubility parameters using the classic Hansen and the DiPEVa method
coredata.dc:creator: Pereira, Claudio Nunes
Paim, Lucas
da Cruz, Joziel Aparecido
Dall Agnol, Lucas
Mauler, Raquel S.
Bianchi, Otávio
coredata.dc:description: Hansen's three-dimensional Solubility Parameter (3DSP) are widely recognized for assessing the compatibility of a dispersed phase with a continuous phase. A modified approach, known as the DiPEVa method, has recently emerged in the literature. This method refines 3DSP by incorporating surface tension considerations. Given the presence of these two similar methodologies, the objective of this study is to conduct a comparative analysis between the two approaches, employing carbon nanoparticles, including graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots (produced via microwave pyrolysis of Spirulina microalgae, and the reaction between citric acid and ethylenediamine), graphene nanoplatelets, and carbon nanotubes as the focal subjects. To achieve this, assessing solubility parameters involved dispersing these nanoparticles in 32 organic solvents with well-established solubility parameters. Subsequently, the collected data underwent a genetic algorithm optimization, facilitating the computation of the Hansen 3D dispersibility sphere. This process included the calculation of the 3DSP for the sphere's central point and its interaction radius (R0). Quality-of-fit metrics, such as DataFit values and the percentage of outliers, were computed to assess the accuracy of the models. As a result, it was evident that the DiPEVa method outperformed the Hansen method in most cases, except for carbon quantum dot dispersion obtained through Spirulina pyrolysis, where both methods yielded comparable results.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hansen's three-dimensional Solubility Parameter (3DSP) are widely recognized for assessing the compatibility of a dispersed phase with a continuous phase. A modified approach, known as the DiPEVa method, has recently emerged in the literature. This method refines 3DSP by incorporating surface tension considerations. Given the presence of these two similar methodologies, the objective of this study is to conduct a comparative analysis between the two approaches, employing carbon nanoparticles, including graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots (produced via microwave pyrolysis of Spirulina microalgae, and the reaction between citric acid and ethylenediamine), graphene nanoplatelets, and carbon nanotubes as the focal subjects. To achieve this, assessing solubility parameters involved dispersing these nanoparticles in 32 organic solvents with well-established solubility parameters. Subsequently, the collected data underwent a genetic algorithm optimization, facilitating the computation of the Hansen 3D dispersibility sphere. This process included the calculation of the 3DSP for the sphere's central point and its interaction radius (R0). Quality-of-fit metrics, such as DataFit values and the percentage of outliers, were computed to assess the accuracy of the models. As a result, it was evident that the DiPEVa method outperformed the Hansen method in most cases, except for carbon quantum dot dispersion obtained through Spirulina pyrolysis, where both methods yielded comparable results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The correct selection of compatible materials is a basic and essential step for achieving successful stable chemical formulations. It is a pre-requisite for solutions, dispersions or emulsions and nanocomposite processing, among other applications. There are several physicochemical properties involved in the preparation of a stable mixture, such as viscosity, surface tension, dipole moment, dielectric constant, density and acid-base interactions, among others. The selection of a solvent mix (solvent and co-solvents) capable of solubilizing the solutes of interest is an important factor for defining the ideal solution [1]. This objective can be achieved using three-dimensional solubility parameters (3DSP), such as Hansen's. These parameters allow for an estimation of a series of properties and are based on a simple concept that similar substances can form good solutions.

The Hansen solubility parameters (HSPs) are numeric constants (δD, δP, δH) associated with properties of chemical bonds and the intermolecular forces of cohesion of the molecules of substances. The dispersion nonpolar forces or induced dipole are represented by δD, polar or permanent dipole forces by δP and hydrogen-bonding by δH. Any substance may be represented by a point (x, y, z) = (δD, δP, δH) in the three-dimensional space, the axes of which are the orthogonal HSP's. In this “Hansen’s space”, a solute is represented by its HSPs and by a radius of interaction R0, thus defining a “sphere of solubility”, within which the substances considered “good solvents” should be inside and substances deemed as “non-solvents” should be outside [2–4].
The Hansen solubility parameters (HSPs) are widely used to predict the affinity of paints, inks, mineral dispersions and other oil and polymer systems [2,3].

The total solubility parameter, also called Hildebrand solubility parameter, is equal to the square root of the total cohesive energy density and can be expressed by [1]:

All Hansen solubility parameters (δD, δP, δH) are related to physicochemical properties through established relationships. Dispersive component and polar components (δD and δP) of HSPs are usually calculated from the refractive index (nD), molar volume (V), dipole moment (μ) and dielectric constant (ε) or by using the Böttcher equation (3), [5–7].

Recently, a 3DSP method called the DiPEVa was proposed by Nunes Pereira et al [8]. These parameters are also related to physicochemical properties in the same way. However, the purpose of these parameters is to obtain more accurate correlations with physicochemical parameters for many more molecules than the Hansen 3DSP. Nevertheless, contrary to HSP, the 3DSP are adjusted for surface tension in the DiPEVa method. This adjustment is performed with the use of a DiPEVa number (DIP #), which is a number resulting from expression that correlates partial solubility parameters in the following manner:

There are many different types of nanoparticles available on the market. Among them, carbon NPs have gained considerable prominence in recent years. Graphene, for example, has generated great interest in several research groups around the world over the last few years due to its distinct physical properties. The potential applications of this material are ample, ranging from polymer composites, energy storage, corrosion inhibition, biological applications, gas-barrier and photonic devices, as exemplified in Fig. 1
[12–17].

These particles can also be used in composites with various polymers, such as acrylics, acrylonitrile, vinyls, polyether ketone (PEEK), polyurethane, epoxy, polystyrene, polycarbonate and polyamide, among others [12,18]. The carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have exceptional mechanical, thermal and electrical properties. However, CNTs are very challenging to disperse homogeneously in most organic solvents and water [19,20]. The other carbon NPs, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), exhibit fluorescence and other unique properties, which allows for their use in applications such as bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, biomedical materials, sensors, printing inks, fingerprinting, photocatalysis and light-emitting diodes [21,22]. In general, improvements in mechanical, thermal, electrical or optical properties are intended. There is no doubt that the properties achieved are related to the degree of dispersion and distribution of the filler in the composite matrix. For each one of these NPs, the knowledge of the 3DSP and the surface tension is a prerequisite, as will be discussed in the present study.

One of the most widespread ways of obtaining these materials with good quality is exfoliation and liquid phase dispersion (relevant for sonication and shear methods), which require the selection of solvents [23]. Large-scale graphene can be obtained by ball milling to exfoliate carbon nanofibers through interaction with solvents. Because of this, several studies have been directed to define the solubility parameters of graphene in order to select the ideal solvent or load to be applied in the process or in the final product [16,24,25].

Solvent interactions with graphene have been rationalized in terms of both surface energies and Hansen solubility parameters; however, there are problems with both these approaches [24–26]. Some molecules considered as good solvents for graphene dispersion are the following: n-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), dimethyl sulfoxide, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), gamma-butyrolactone (GBL) and H2O, in descendant order of effectiveness [25]. Other studies refer to the use of ball-milling to exfoliate graphite in a wide variety of organic solvents, including molecules such as ethanol, formamide, acetone, tetrahydrofuran (THF), tetramethyl urea (TMU), DMF and NMP. These authors proposed that the solvents DMF, NMP and TMU are the good solvents, having the surface tensions matching that of graphene sheets [24]. Additionally, ecofriendly solvents could be used for the purpose of graphene dispersion and exfoliation, such as the Cyrene® molecule. Neither the solubility parameter nor the surface tension can be used exclusively to screen solvents for use on carbon NPs [15,27]. Thus, in the present study we intend to use 3DSP adjusted to surface tension to discover if it has a better predictive value for predicting dispersibility of carbon NPs.

From the view of thermodynamics, the compatibility of filler and polymer matrix dominates filler dispersion and filler-matrix interactions. As a consequence, it is significant for us to study the compatibility of graphene or other NPs and each possible polymer applied to composites [17].

The aim of the present study is to compare carbon NP dispersion using two different 3DSP schemes. Five carbon nanoparticle representatives were selected: graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots (obtained by microwave pyrolysis of spirulina microalgae or reaction between citric acid and ethylenediamine), graphene nanoplatelets and carbon nanotubes. The methods selected to evaluate the dispersion were the gold standard HSP and the novel alternative called DiPEVa solubility parameters. The results obtained for each method included the center of the dispersion sphere, in solubility parameter values, the interaction radius of the sphere, the DataFit, which is the traditional measure of accuracy of the HSP, and outliers percentage data. The optimization of the solubility parameters utilized were the classic HSP and the DiPEVa method, previously described in the literature, aiming towards DataFit maximization [2–4,8,28]. It is expected that a comparison can be made of the accuracy of the results of each method in terms of carbon NPs dispersibility, represented by dispersibility spheres, measured in solubility parameters. A better carbon NP dispersibility in solvents could be useful in the design of improved preparation routes for nanocomposites and others applications using carbon NPs.

The analyzed materials were five different carbon NPs (graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots (obtained by microwave pyrolysis of spirulina microalgae and by reaction between citric acid and ethylenediamine), graphene nanoplatelets, and carbon nanotubes). The first three were used as obtained and are described below, while the last two were produced by the authors; and the process will be detailed below.

(a) Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized with low defect density from natural graphite by modified Hummer's method [29,30]. The material was obtained from the Centre for Advanced 2D Materials (CA2DM), National University of Singapore, 6 Science Drive 2, Singapore 117546.

(b) Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs – CAS number 308068–56-6, arc-produced, diameter = 2–50 nm, length ≥ 2 µm (55–65 wt% nanotubes). Supplied by Strem Chemicals, Inc. 7 Mulliken Way, Newburyport, MA 01950, USA.

(c) Graphene nanoplatelets (CAS number 1034343–98-0, with 6–8 nm in thickness and 25 μm in diameter, bulk density 0.3–1 g/cm3, surface area within 120–150 m2/g, with > 99.5 % C, <1% O and residual acidic content of 0.5 %, in accordance with the manufacture information) were obtained from Strem Chemicals, Inc. 7 Mulliken Way, Newburyport, MA 01950, USA.

(d) The CQDs (1) were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of CA in presence of EDA [22]. In brief, CA and EDA with 1:1 M ratio were dissolved in 10 mL distilled water. The mixture was sonicated for 2 min to form a transparent solution. The solution was then irradiated for 6 min in a domestic microwave oven (650 W). The resulting red-brown solid was dissolved and dialyzed against pure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 100) for 3 days. After dialysis, the obtained solution was lyophilized to yield powdered CQDs.

(e) The CQDs (2) were synthesized by pyrolysis of Spirulina biomass (dried-powder) (purchased from Galena Química e Farmacêutica Ltda, Campinas, Brazil) at 300 °C for 2 h under a heating rate of 10 °C‧min−1 [21]. After carbonization, the fine powder was dispersed in water (20 mL), and then centrifuged at 13000 rpm for fifteen minutes to remove suspended solid impurities. Finally, the CQDs were obtained by evaporation of the solvent.
All solvents were of analytical grade, and all other chemicals were used as received. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ cm) was used in all the experiments.

Table 1 shows the organic compounds selected for the study. Molar volume (V) and the Hansen solubility parameters (δD, δP, δH) were taken from the Hansen solubility parameters handbook [3]. Experimental surface tension was taken from Jasper’s study [33]. The DiPEVa parameters were taken from previous studies or calculated for the present study using the described method [8].

The carbon NP dispersibility was evaluated using the Hansen solubility method. The dispersibility state of each carbon NP in every evaluating solvent was used to build the 3D dispersibility sphere utilizing an optimization algorithm previously validated method [2]. Selection of the best individual 3DSP after several algorithm iteration cycles (the evolution), is guided according to the purpose of the DataFit maximization. Therefore, the DataFit value was the chosen individual fitness parameter. This evolutionary process repeats cyclically until DataFit is higher than a tolerance factor (based on previous tests). In the end, the best individual gives ideal solubility parameters, radius of the sphere, and outliers’ percentage for the carbon NP dispersion. To keep the dispersibility sphere radius as small as possible, a small size factor to DataFit was multiplied, decreasing it as a function of R0, as described in the cited study [2].

The input data for the optimization processes is presented in Table 2 . A list of 32 solvents for which there are experimental data and a correlation (i = 1, 2, …, n) with its known 3DSPs (δDi, δPi, δHi), either Hansen's or DiPEVa SP, and its dispersibility factors (Di = 0, 1). The dispersibility states are dispersible, partially dispersible or not dispersible, which indicates the degree of interaction between the nanoparticle, and each solvent was selected. Zero is assigned to non-dispersant or partially dispersant, while 1 is attributed to the dispersant solvents. For carbon nanoparticle dispersibility, a constant concentration of the solute was diluted to the dispersant solvents, comprising 32 different organic solvents comprising various classes of solvents. The database includes protic to aprotic molecules, linear to branched, cyclic, aromatic, small and larger molecules, water, and polyhydric alcohols. The physicochemical parameters of each solvent used to calculate the DIPEVa solubility parameter are also shown in Table 2. The parameters included were the surface tension at 25 °C (mN.m−1), dipole moment (μ), experimental dielectric constant (εr), the ratio of the coordination number (Zs.Z-
1), and the parameters derived from them.

According to the literature, graphene oxide has several functional groups, such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl, and carbonyl [29,30]. There is no description from the CNT manufacturer about the functional groups present. Regarding the graphene nanoplatelets, according to the supplier, the following functional groups are present: amine, imine, carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxyl, pyrone, and lactone. According to the literature, CQDs have hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and amide functional groups [21,22]. It is known that the surface functional groups can determine the solubility properties of the nanoparticles [34,35]. Many types of surface modification techniques based on mechanical milling, covalent bonding of functional groups or non-covalent adsorption of mediating molecules have been suggested to improve the dispersibility, mostly in solvent media [35]. Therefore, the 3DSP of the carbon NPs depends upon the functional groups present in the particles. Functional groups can be characterized and quantified by techniques such as XPS, FTIR and NMR, which, however, are limited in providing information about the solubility behavior in real environments. Determining the 3DSP of the carbon NPs is a practical and qualitative way to explore the presence of functional groups, which respond proportionally and provide the nanofiller's solubility characteristics.

As outlined in the materials and methods section, the sedimentation behavior was evaluated through visual observation following a 10-minute ultrasonication period, with subsequent resting of the sample for 120 h. It's important to note that complete sedimentation did not occur within shorter time frames for certain samples. This implies that the method employed may exhibit lower sensitivity during these shorter intervals. Consequently, this limitation might result in many solvents appearing as effective dispersants without effectively distinguishing the superior dispersants from those that only partially promote dispersion.

Choosing a diverse range of solvents is a critical aspect of this study. The number of solvents employed for this purpose can vary significantly, ranging from as few as 14 to well over 40 solvents in different research investigations. In several articles, the calculation of Hansen solubility parameters has been carried out using a selection of solvents that typically fall within the range of 14 to 25 solvents [21,31,32,36–41]. However, a few researchers, in addition to the guidance provided in the Hansen Handbook, have opted to work with a more extensive set of 40 solvents or even more [3,20,26]. The quantity of solvents utilized in the present study aligns with established practices in the existing literature.

The evaluations encompassed a variety of chemical functionalities, including hydroxyl, ester, ether, carbonyl, carboxyl, amide, and nitrile groups. These functionalities are associated with high-cohesion energy and exhibit characteristics such as a high dielectric constant and dipole moment, as seen in acids, alcohols, amines, and amides [3]. Fig. 3 illustrates the relationship between the association component of the solubility parameter (δa), which is the summation of squared polar and hydrogen bond parameters, and the dielectric constant. This parameter serves as a polarity scale, effectively integrating crucial solubility parameters. Solvents with low cohesion energy tend to have lower values for the association solubility parameter component (δa), while high cohesion energy solvents, like acids, alcohols, amines, and amides, exhibit higher values. Fig. 3 provides an overview of solvent distribution within this scale, which encapsulates both categories of solvents.
The dispersibility factors (Di = 0, 1), either dispersible, partially dispersible or not dispersible, obtained from dispersion experiments are shown in Table 2.

After 50 iterations of the evolutionary algorithm program, the 3DSPs center of spheres best values were determined along with the highest DataFit and the lowest R0. The results achieved with the optimization method are presented in Table 3 :

As a general trend, the interaction radius tended to be smaller in the HSP method. This was expected because water-soluble solvents usually have higher polar (δP) and hydrogen-bond (δH) parameters when compared to the HSP. In many cases, as in the case of the polar aprotic, such as dimethyl sulfoxide and dimethylformamide or the glycols and water, the dispersive component (δD) of the 3DSP is also higher when compared to the nonpolar and alcohol molecules. Probably because of this method's particularity, these interaction radiuses tended to be higher.

The 3DSP is conceptually derived from a physicochemical background. The parameters are related to empirical data such as enthalpies, molar volume, dipole moment, dielectric constant, surface tension, and its components [2,3,8,28]. The DiPEVa 3DSP is related to these physicochemical parameters with high accuracy. In the following figure, the associating (δa) component of solubility parameters is compared to the physicochemical parameters using the database of the present study.

The polar component of the solubility parameter (δP) is linearly correlated to the dipole moment and squared volume product (μ.V -1/2). The linear formula is y = 0.0531x-0.0137. R2 = 0.9998 (98.98 %). This correlation is equivalent to that obtained in the HSP method.

The association component of the solubility parameter (δa) was also highly correlated to the dielectric constant in the present database, as shown in Fig. 3 in the DiPEVa method. This relationship was first described for the HSP in Hansen’s team, but was not adjusted for the ratio of the coordination number [36]. The physicochemical parameters dipole moment and dielectric constant are related to the solvent polarity. As discussed below, they are essential for the carbon NPs dispersion, particularly in some particles. Using the DiPEVa method strengthens the precision of the polarity assessment in these cases.

Physicochemical parameters, such as the dipole moment and the dielectric constant, are relevant for microwave processing methods. These parameters are also crucial for nanoplatelet exfoliation, for example. Thus, a method that has a better correlation with the dielectric constant and the dipole moment is essential in these applications.

The surface tension was not compared because, due to the optimization process of the DiPEVa method, the relationship is almost 100 % [8,28]. It is known that surface tension is also important for nanodispersion applications [8,12–14,16,19,37–39]. In the specific case of the DiPEVA method, the 3DSPs are adjusted for surface tension. Thus, we suggest further studies to deepen these dispersion analyses, particularly in applications using water-based solvents, in the knowledge that HSPs were developed initially for oily solvent-based paint applications. The authors, therefore, propose that the method is relevant to applications that rely not only on the surface or interfacial phenomena but also on dielectric properties, such as microwave processing and nanoplatelet exfoliation [40,41].

The Hansen solubility parameters vary for each carbon NP, even within different production methods of the same carbon NPs, and also due to chemical modification and functionalization [35]. It is, therefore, important to calculate the HSP values of the material used in every specific research, although general trends are also valuable. Few articles include the HSP calculated experimentally for carbon NPs. Through a survey of the literature, the following HSP values were obtained. The HSP for graphene oxide were found to be (δD = 16.50, δP = 13.50, δH = 26.60, δT = 34.10) and for rGO (δD = 17.60, δP = 9.17, δH = 9.05, δT = 21.90) in MPa1/2 [42]. In the present study, only graphene oxide was tested. The results obtained from the Hansen and DiPEVa methods were very compatible. The results were also comparable to the literature, although the cited article proposed a higher value for the hydrogen-bond parameter (δH = 26.60 vs. 10.15 or 7.14 MPa1/2, respectively). The obtained result in this study was more compatible with the literature rGO (See Table 3), which could mean that the NPs tested in this study had fewer polar functional groups [42]. In this specific case, the DiPEVa achieved a higher DataFit (0.83 vs. 0.95), which means it is more accurate in this database. Contrary to the graphene compound, graphene oxide (GO) can form stable solutions in water and some organic solvents. This is possible through the strong hydrogen bonding between GO functional groups and solvent molecules. The hydrogen bond energy between GO functional groups and solvent molecules decreases in the sequence: water > methanol > ethanol [37]. In this case, the better fit of the alcohols and water molecules in the DiPEVa model may have benefitted this model, which achieved a higher DataFit value. The improved optimization could result from the high correlation association solubility parameter (δa) to the dielectric moment.

After a broad study comprising analysis of 40 solvents, it was suggested that the good solvents for graphene should have an HSP close to δD = 18.0 MPa1/2, δP = 9.3 MPa1/2, and δH = 7.7 MPa1/2 [15]. These solubility parameters suggested cyclopentanone and cyclohexanone as good solvents for graphene dispersibility. But chloroform and isopropanol, which have low boiling points, could also be used [26]. It is worth noting that this value for graphene is similar to that cited previously for rGO [42]. This was expected because the rGO has fewer functional groups due to the process applied to its production. Good solvents for graphene have 15 MPa1/2 < δD < 21 MPa1/2, δP ∼ 12 MPa1/2 and δH ∼9 MPa1/2, respectively. Despite its apparent nonpolar nature, the suitable solvents for graphene exfoliation have non-zero values of δP and δH [26,43]. This premise that the nonpolar molecules have zero δP and δH, as the alkanes case in the HSP case, does not apply to the DIPEVa concept. Authors Coleman and Hernandez and colleagues also pointed out this apparent contradiction and suggested there was inadequacy in the HSP [26,43]. Besides this, both Son et al and Park et al. reported that graphene dispersion was related to the δP and δH values [44,45]. The results of the graphene nanoplatelets calculated in this study with HSP were very similar: δD = 18.08 MPa1/2, δP = 9.66 MPa1/2, and δH = 8.48 MPa1/2. Other than these, the values obtained in the DiPEVa method were different: δD = 18.26 MPa1/2, δP = 19.91 MPa1/2, and δH = 1.0 MPa1/2. These results deserve some consideration. Firstly, the DataFit of the DiPEVa is higher than that of HSP (0.69 vs. 0.90), while the outliers are smaller (18 vs. 15.6 %). Both these results are better in the case of the DiPEVa method; therefore, the DiPEVa in this case was more accurate. On the other hand, the interaction radius is smaller in the HSP case (5.38 vs. 17.95). What could be the reason for this finding? The DiPEVa solubility parameters of water-miscible molecules with high dipole moment and dielectric constant are higher than the traditional HSP. This is particularly true for polar molecules' polar (δP) and hydrogen-bond parameters (δH). The dispersive component (δD) in the water-miscible molecules, such as polyhydric alcohols, water, polar aprotic solvents such as DMF and DMSO, and glycols are also higher. Therefore, the larger interaction radius is due to these individuals' higher 3DSP. In this case, the method's precision, the DataFit, and outliers are desirable factors because they are quality-of-fit measures, not the smaller interaction radius.

In many applications that require the combination of functional properties that each carbon NP has, it is possible to combine them. For example, carbon dots can be used to obtain anti-fraud devices, while graphene oxide results in good mechanical properties in polar polymers. Carbon nanotubes can result in good electrical properties. Thus, the combination of multiple particles can result in functional materials.

Knowing the 3DSP that dissolves a polymer or disperses an inorganic filler is desirable because this knowledge can be used to select the solvents applied in the formulation or production method. Additionally, an additive, such as a plasticizer, an antioxidant, a compatibilizer or a UV protector, can be best selected with the knowledge of the polymer, filler, and the polymer additive 3DSP. Therefore, understanding these 3DSP is essential to obtain composite polymer material.

This is also true for Hybrid material, comprising two different nanoparticle fillers, in which the knowledge of the 3DSP of each material is also beneficial. For example, the Hansen sphere of dispersibility presented in Table 3 can be applied to predict this hybrid nanoparticle mixture. Based on the obtained DiPEVa values, it could be mixed graphene oxide and Spirulina nanoparticles, or graphene nanoplatelets and carbon nanotubes. In both hybrid materials, in theory, good miscibility can be predicted by the resemblance of the calculated 3DSP.

This discussion will focus on the two carbon NPs with the most disparate 3DSP results. Regarding the false solvents obtained in the optimization, in the case of carbon dots, the HSP received the following solvents: diethyl ether, butyl acetate, ethyl acetate, toluene, methyl acetate, phenylmethanol, and water. On the other hand, the DiPEVa method misjudged 1,2-dichoroethane, but did not misjudge either phenylmethanol or water. In the other solvents, there was agreement. The correct indication of water is essential because it is considered the universal solvent, and there is a migration to water-based technologies due to environmental concerns. The water solvent has at least three different HSP values, which is so problematic that it deserves a separate chapter in the Hansen Handbook of solubility parameters [3,46]. For this reason, calculating the solubility sphere is suggested not to include H2O in the computation [3,47].

Regarding graphene nanoplatelets, the Hansen method misjudged diethylene glycol and 1,2-dichloroethene, while the DiPEVa method misjudged methyl ethyl ketone. In the other solvents, there was agreement. In this second case, it is also worth noting the correct appointing of the diethylene glycol solvent, which is also water miscible.

For these reasons, the present study affirmed that the DiPEVa method was more correct in evaluating water-miscible solvents and water itself than the HSP, having a higher DataFit value in these cases. The water-miscible solvents have higher polar (δP) and larger hydrogen-bond (δH) solubility parameters than the oil-miscible ones. And it also justifies the larger interaction radius to include these solvents.

Due to these considerations, the present study demonstrated that the DiPEVa solubility parameters, in addition to having an excellent correlation with the physicochemical parameters, including dielectric constant and surface tension, classify better water-based solvents in their optimizations [8,28]. As these solvents tend to be preferred due to the preservation of the environment, the method is relevant for science and industry.

Several spheres are presented in the following figures to elucidate the 3D sphere of the various carbon NPs. The 3D and 2D dispersibility spheres of all carbon NPs were drawn. They are shown in Fig. 4 below.

Fig. 4 shows that in the dispersibility spheres, obtained both in the HSP and the DiPEVa parameters, the CQDs (1) have a much larger interaction radius than the other carbon NPs. In the other carbon NPs, the HSP method generated very similar spheres. In the DiPEVa method, GO and CQD (2) had similar spheres, while CNT and GNP had the closest spheres.

The carbon NP dispersibility spheres obtained agree with the literature, which showed very polar HSP results in polar (δP) and hydrogen bonding (δH) components. Bergin and colleagues proposed the following HSP for carbon nanotube dispersion: δD = 17.8 MPa1/2, δP = 7.5 MPa1/2, and δH = 7.6 MPa1/2 [39].

For the dispersion of carbon nanotubes, Detrich and collaborators found the following HSP: δD = 19 MPa1/2, δP = 8 MPa1/2, δH = 4 MPa1/2, respectively [20]. Due to the difficulty in dispersing CNT, the solubility of CNTs is often carried out by surface modification, which leads to different HSPs depending on the type of modification [35]. In the present study, the DiPEVa method found a higher DataFit compared to the HSP method, with the following 3DSP results: δD = 18.15 MPa1/2, δP = 16.01 MPa1/2, δH = 4.38 MPa1/2 (Table 3). The results of the other carbon NPs will be graphically evaluated.

Fig. 5 compares the dispersibility spheres with both methods. In this specific case, the dispersibility sphere of the DiPEVa method was more significant than the HSP. On the other hand, DataFit was higher, which shows greater accuracy. In the 2D projection, one can see two suitable solvents outside the HSP sphere (solvents 3 and 5), which are false negatives of the method. The same solvents are practically superimposed on the boundary of the DiPEVa sphere, showing their better positioning. This positioning is that which resulted in a higher DataFit.

In the present study, as in the case of CNT, the literature also points out that polar solvents are suitable solvents for graphene NPs. Concerning this, it is known that the generated dispersibility spheres depend on the solvents used. A large group of solvents were used, which is positive. Despite this, there are studies with a larger number of solvents tested, e.g., the cited large study, comprising 40 solvents, which suggested that the good solvents for graphene should have an HSP close to δD = 18.0 MPa1/2, δP = 9.3 MPa1/2, and δH = 7.7 MPa1/2 [15].

Other studies show similar results. Good solvents for graphene have 15 MPa1/2 < δD < 21 MPa1/2, δP ∼ 12 MPa1/2 and δH ∼9 MPa1/2, respectively. Despite its apparent nonpolar nature, the good solvents for graphene exfoliation have non-zero values of δP and δH [26,43].

For the specific case of graphene nanofibers dispersion, a value of hydrogen-bonding Hansen parameter was proposed after testing a large set of solvents (δH∼ 14–16 MPa1/2) [48]. It was also suggested that effective solvents for the graphene had surface tensions close to 40 mN.m−1 [26], or between 25 and 35 mN.m−1 in the case of platelet-type graphite nanofibers[48]. For platelet-type graphite nanofiber dispersion, the solvents 1-butanol, ethanol, allyl alcohol or cyclohexanol could also be applied. Carbon nanofibers were not evaluated in the present study.

These results from the literature show that graphene NPs are very polar, compatible with the present study, or generate larger dispersibility spheres. Despite the use of an extensive database in the present study, unfortunately, it was of insufficient size for polar solvents, such as some of the alcohols mentioned in the referred studies, and especially solvents such as GBL and NMP, which are considered good solvents for dispersing graphene. Likewise, cyclopentanone or cyclohexanone solvents, described in the literature, could also have been tested. The inclusion of these solvents would likely modify the dispersibility sphere.

It was also decided to consider dispersants as only solvents that completely dissolved the NP, not those that partially dissolved it. These were considered non-dispersing. This may have interfered with the composition of the dispersibility sphere, but it is a consolidated approach in the HSP technique. Therefore, we believe the present results from both methods are compatible with the literature.

Figs. 6 and 7
show the results of the CQD evaluation and comparison between 3D and 2D spheres of CQD (1) and CQD (2). The case of the HSP method is shown in Fig. 6.

CQDs(1) have a much larger interaction radius in the HSP method, meaning they have greater dispersibility in polar solvents, such as water-miscible, with high δP and/or δH components. The same pattern in the DiPEVa parameters is shown in Fig. 7.

CQD (2) has equivalent quality-of-fit in both cases, showing high accuracy (DataFit = 1). And the 3DSP is also similar in both methods. In the case of CQD (1), the DataFit was higher in the case of the HSP method. Regarding the literature, in the case of CQD (2), 3DSP characteristics of polar solvents were also obtained.

For the case of Spirulina quantum dots, the values obtained in the literature were δD = 19.21 MPa1/2, δP = 9.76 MPa1/2, δH = 7.43 MPa1/2 and R0 = 7.619, respectively [21]. These results agree well with the present study's results (see Table 3). Both the HSP and DiPEVa results were almost equivalent.

In this case, CQD (1) is expected to have greater dispersibility in polar solvents, while CQD (2) would be more interesting for hybrid materials using non-water-soluble polymers and less polar chemical actives.

In summary, the data obtained are following the literature. The larger dispersibility spheres in the case of DiPEVa may be due to the factors mentioned above. Except for CQD (1), the carbon NPs obtained very similar dispersibility spheres in the case of HSP, which shows a lack of distinction between them. The DiPEVa method distinguished the other 4 carbon NPs into two groups, or one group and two NPs distinct from the others.

The GNP results are in line with the literature qualitatively. As the literature points out, despite the common sense regarding expecting dispersibility of graphene NPs by low polar solvents, this, following the literature, was not what was obtained in the present study either. Thus, this information can be confirmed as empirical. There is no standard production of carbon NPs; therefore, the functional groups of the same NP from different origins are very different. This is another factor to show the distinction between different works and different solvents tested. DiPEVa's difference for these carbon NPs with the tested solvents is essential to point out possible formulations of hybrid materials with one or two other NPs fillers. The higher DataFit results obtained and, in several cases, a lower percentage of outliers, with the DiPEVa approach found in the present study, are noteworthy.

From Figs. 4-7, it can be seen that the dispersibility sphere using the DIPEVa method is larger. However, essential solvents such as the water-miscible and even polar aprotic such as dimethyl sulfoxide and dimethyl formamide are correctly classified closer to the dispersibility spheres. These solvents are important for dissolving polar polymers such as polyurethane, polycarbonates, vinylic etc. This graphically corroborates with what was discussed concerning the misclassified solvents presented.

The same algorithm for optimizing the 3DSP values ​​was used to estimate the solubility parameters of several polymers of interest in the literature. These data will be compared with the values of carbon NPs presented in this study. Data from the 3D sphere of polymer dissolution is shown in Table 4 below.

According to the literature, stable graphene dispersions can be prepared only if the surface tension and the Hildebrand solubility parameters of the solvents and diluents match the graphene parameters. Surfactants are also added to prepare stable graphene dispersions. Graphene dispersions were designed with several different surfactants and solvents, such as NMP, EtOH, IPA, water, and DCM, with a short sonication time (3 h) [23].

Polymer functionalization or block copolymer association can be used to achieve stable graphene dispersions in polymers, solvent and/or surfactant dispersion techniques. Copolymers used include polar monomers such as PVP, PEG, or PEO. Solvents such as EtOH, NMP, DCM, THF, and/or various surfactants are also used in these cases. Surfactants can be used alone but are usually associated with a cosurfactant [23]. In such cases, HSPs can be used to calculate the dispersibility sphere of nanofillers such as rGO functionalized with surfactants [47]. In all these composites or hybrid composites cases, it is essential to evaluate and match each composite compound's 3DSP and surface tension, thus demonstrating the importance of the present study.

The following polymers are described in the literature, forming composites with graphene: acrylics, acrylonitrile, vinyl, polyether ketone (PEEK), polyurethane, epoxy, polystyrene, polyester, elastomer, polycarbonate, ethyl cellulose, and polyamide [12,17,18,23,44]. Corroborating literature data, Table 4 shows that PMMA, epoxy, PVC, PS, polyamide, or elastomers containing nitrile or styrene groups are compatible with graphene. Less polar elastomers, such as butyl, isoprene and isobutylene groups, not associated with polar monomer, would not supposedly be compatible with graphene. Table 4 shows that the PMMA, epoxy, PVC, PS, and NBR are the more polar polymers. At the same time, PCL and polyamide are intermediaries to nonpolar elastomers, such as isoprene, butylene, and butyl rubber. Luo and collaborators studied the molecular dynamics between the SBR elastomer and graphene [17]. Through the present study, it is expected that the styrene content of the copolymer is the solubility factor.

It is worth noting that the calculated 3DSP depends on other factors besides the monomer type. The PCL dispersion results show that the 3DSP parameters depend on the polymer molecular weight and the dispersion concentration (See Table 4).

As previously discussed, based on the obtained DiPEVa values, it could be mixed graphene oxide and Spirulina nanoparticles or graphene nanoplatelets and carbon nanotubes. For the first hybrid composite combining graphene oxide and spirulina CQD, polystyrene and PVC could be the polymers to use. For the second hybrid, graphene nanoplatelets and carbon nanotubes, highly polar polymers such as PMMA, epoxy and NBR could be tried. Carbon dots could also be compatible with polar polymers like epoxy or PMMA. The low polar elastomers could enhance the compatibility for graphene oxide and significantly reduce graphene oxide (not tested in the present study but less polar than the graphene oxide) and Spirulina CQD. It should be highlighted that the suggestions are preliminary because coupling agents, surfactants, polymer functionalization, and copolymerization could be used to enhance polymer compatibility.

Carbon nanoparticle dispersibility was assessed across 32 solvents employing both the HSP and DiPEVa 3DSP methods. An analysis of dispersibility concerning solubility parameters demonstrated that the DiPEVa method offered superior precision compared to the Hansen scheme. Nevertheless, both methods generated congruent results when examining graphene oxide and the dispersion of carbon dots from Spirulina.

The findings unveiled in this study have broad applications in various fields utilizing nanomaterials, thereby enhancing their processing for applications in medicine, sensor technology, composite materials, and many other domains. Solubility parameters function as valuable tools for establishing safer and environmentally conscious pathways for nanoparticle utilization, including selecting more eco-friendly solvents and optimizing particle functionalities.
